label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. The question is about a study by the World Bank in 1974 regarding economic growth and development in some backward countries. The passage says that in the early 1970s, the relationship between growth and development was being questioned, and the 1974 World Bank study concluded that "more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." Wait, that wording is a bit tricky. Let me parse it again. The exact sentence is: "A major study by the World Bank in 1974 concluded that it is now clear that more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." So, the study found that even after many years of rapid growth, only a third of the population (so, about 33%) had benefited. That means the growth didn't actually benefit most of the population. The options are: A) benefit to a third of their population B) benefit to two thirds of their population C) little benefit to their people D) no benefit at all to their people Let me check again. The passage says that the growth was of little benefit to a third of their population. Wait, that wording is confusing. If the growth was of little benefit to a third, that means the rest (two thirds) did get some benefit? Or does it mean that even the third didn't get much? Let me read the exact sentence again carefully. Original text: "A major study by the World Bank in 1974 concluded that it is now clear that more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." Wait, that's phrased as the growth has been of little benefit to a third. So the growth didn't help a third much, but what about the rest? The next sentence says that the increase in GNP per capita did not promise important improvements in nutrition, health, education. So overall, the GNP growth didn't translate to development for most. But the study specifically says "little benefit to a third", implying that two-thirds might have had some benefit? Or is there a misinterpretation here? Wait, maybe it's phrased as "has been of little benefit to a third of their population" meaning that for a third of the population, the growth was of little benefit. That would imply that the rest of the population (two-thirds) might have benefited more. But the next sentence says that the benefit came down to only a small part of the population. So maybe the study's conclusion is that only a small part (maybe the third mentioned) actually benefited, and the rest didn't. Let me see the exact wording again. The passage says: "A major study by the World Bank in 1974 concluded that it is now clear that more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." Wait, but that's phrased as "has been of little benefit to a third of their population." So the growth was of little benefit to a third. But that's not the same as saying that the growth had little benefit for most. Let me check the next sentence: "The World Bank study showed that increase in GNP per capita did not promise important improvements in such development indicators as nutrition, health, and education.
